---
layout: post
title: "E-mail etiquette for teachers"
date: 2016-04-16
---

<span></span><br />
<div style="-en-clipboard: true; text-align: center;">
<a href="https://www.blogger.com/blogger.g?blogID=9145285415158126914" imageanchor="1" style="clear: left; float: left; margin-bottom: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"></a><img src="data:image/png;base64," style="height: auto;" /></div>
<div style="text-align: center;">
<span style="font-size: 11px;"><span style="word-wrap: break-word;"><span style="-webkit-line-break: after-white-space; font-size: 11px;">Source:</span></span></span> <span style="word-wrap: break-word;"><span style="-webkit-line-break: after-white-space;"><a href="http://adigaskell.org/" style="font-size: 11px;">http://adigaskell.org/</a></span></span></div>
<div>
<br /></div>
<div>
When I started teaching back in 1999 e-mail was the new cool thing in schools. In the 17 years or so since I have seen some hilarious things. Near the top of my list come:</div>
<ul>
<li><div>
an e-mail from a deputy head on e-mail etiquette that was itself littered with typos (and not in an ironic way!)</div>
</li>
<li><div>
the history department being branded the 'shitory' department following a series of unfortunate typos</div>
</li>
<li><div>
a reply to all in which an-under-the-table deal between the director of sport and a teacher who rather fancied being free on Wednesday afternoons came to light: 'I thought we'd agreed you'd say I was doing badminton!'</div>
</li>
</ul>
<div>
In last week's<a href="http://tidepodcast.org/post/138944219782/tide-episode-38">&nbsp;TIDE podcast</a> Doug Belshaw and Dai Barnes discussed some of the ins-and-outs of e-mail etiquette for teachers. Here is what they suggested, along with some things of my own that I've picked up over the years:</div>
<ol>
<li><div>
Reply to all e-mails addressed directly to you within 24 hours - even if just with a holding response. Oh, if only a few more people did this... E-mail is like marking: if you don't keep on top of it you'll soon get buried. Little and often is the key. I personally suggest doing it in blocks or else it has the potential to swamp you with 'busy work'. Cal Newport makes a very useful distinction between 'hard work' and 'hard to do work' <a href="http://calnewport.com/blog/2011/11/11/if-youre-busy-youre-doing-something-wrong-the-surprisingly-relaxed-lives-of-elite-achievers/">here</a> which is worth a read in this regard.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Treat e-mails from pupils and your direct reports with exactly the same courtesy as you do those of your own line managers.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Try using smileys to convey cheerfulness. I wouldn't suggest doing this to parents, or in e-mails which need to convey an element of formality, but I would suggest doing it in almost all other situations. I started doing this a year or so ago. It can change an e-mail that would otherwise seem curt - even rude - into something much more palatable -:)</div>
</li>
<li><div>
In a similar vein, always be unfailingly courteous in your e-mails. Too many people rub others up in the wrong way with careless use of words. If you need to have a 'difficult conversation' have it face to face. Then, and only then, if you must, follow up with an e-mail confirming in a matter-of-fact way what was discussed.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
When e-mailing parents always start off with formal forms of address. Thereafter I have found the sooner you can get on first name terms the better. A useful technique I learnt years ago is to suffix their first name with an ('if I may'). As in Dear Angela (if I may)... This is a good way of making the leap to first names without sounding presumptuous (no-one has ever replied to me 'no you may not'!)</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Avoid Bcc in almost all instances. It is especially underhand to Bcc in a line-manager when e-mailing one of your team. Be open, be honest.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
One situation where you might like to adopt Bcc is to remove someone from a discussion which they started, but which they no longer need to be part of. As in: 'moving you to Bcc now to save your inbox..' Moving someone to the Bcc field in this way means they are left out of subsequent replies to a thread. I've not tried this yet, but it makes sense.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Think carefully about other people's inboxes - do you really need to send a particular message to all the staff? If little Jonny has lost his scarf and sending a message to all is the only way of dealing with the issue you really need to sort out a different channel for your school. At Oswestry, we use a Google sites announcement page to which I have appended a script that scrapes all the messages from the last 24hrs into an e-mail digest, sent to anyone who's subscribed.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Do check for typos. You are a teacher - an e-mail littered with misspellings gives a bad impression. I use <a href="https://app.grammarly.com/">Grammarly</a> which does a pretty good job of saving me from the worst blushes.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Never respond to an angry e-mail. Ring the person up or arrange to meet them in person. A shouting match in BOLD TYPE is a waste of everyone's time.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
Don't send e-mails late on a Friday night after you've had a few drinks. Wait until Saturday.</div>
</li>
</ol>
<div>
Do you have any other tips you'd add to these? If so, I'd love to hear from you!</div>
<br />
